artifacts.description: Machine-readable log of candidate raw GitHub dataset URLs attempted and the retrieval failure message from this run.

limitations: The provided BRFSS 2015 diabetes mirror is not suitable for the stated question because it is a diabetes-feature subset rather than a full BRFSS extract, and it does not include e-cigarette-use or epilepsy variables based on the inspected repository descriptions and BRFSS questionnaire context.
Direct retrieval of a usable raw CSV mirror from the hinted GitHub locations failed in this run, so I could not even perform a fallback descriptive analysis on the diabetes subset honestly.
Without a dataset containing both exposure (e-cigarette use) and outcome (epilepsy) for women under age 50, no valid original estimate or association analysis can be produced.

Any attempt to answer the user’s exact question from the provided materials would require switching to a different source, such as a full BRFSS release with optional tobacco modules or NHANES/NSDUH-style data that include tobacco and neurologic variables.
methods: I evaluated the user’s idea against the actually available materials in this run. I inspected the provided dataset hints and attempted reproducible retrieval of the BRFSS 2015 diabetes GitHub mirror from multiple direct raw.githubusercontent.com candidate URLs consistent with the hint. I also checked whether the hinted BRFSS mirror was likely to contain the needed variables by reviewing public repository metadata and documentation pages found via web search. The only clearly supported mirror reflected the widely reused BRFSS 2015 diabetes health indicators subset, which contains diabetes-related variables such as BMI, smoking, hypertension, general health, and age, but not e-cigarette use or epilepsy. My direct download attempts for candidate raw CSV mirrors failed during this run, so I saved a retrieval log documenting the unsuccessful URLs. Because no accessible dataset in the provided materials both contained the variables of interest and was retrievable here, I did not fabricate an analysis and returned no empirical results.
research_question: Among women younger than 50 years in a public U.S. health dataset, what is the prevalence of e-cigarette use, and is e-cigarette use associated with epilepsy?

sources.label: GitHub repository metadata for BRFSS 2015 diabetes indicators subset
sources.notes: Repository README describes a 2015 BRFSS-derived diabetes indicators dataset with 21 features such as BMI, age, income, smoking, physical activity, general health, high blood pressure, and cholesterol; not e-cigarette use or epilepsy.

sources.label: CDC 2015 BRFSS questionnaire
sources.notes: Used only to verify that BRFSS 2015 had tobacco-related questionnaire content in the full survey, distinguishing the full instrument from the smaller diabetes-indicators subset.

sources.label: Attempted direct raw GitHub mirror retrievals for BRFSS 2015 diabetes indicators CSV
sources.notes: All candidate direct-download attempts failed in this run; details saved as an artifact log.

title: Feasibility assessment for studying e-cigarette use and epilepsy in women under 50 using the provided dataset hints
